title: Pregnancy Calculator — Due Date, Weeks Pregnant & Trimester
description: Calculate your due date, current week of pregnancy, trimester, and key milestones. Based on last menstrual period or conception date. Free pregnancy tracker.
ymyl_disclaimer: This calculator provides estimates based on Naegele's rule. Due dates are estimates — only 4% of babies are born on the calculated date. Always consult your healthcare provider for accurate pregnancy dating and prenatal care.

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.q1: How is the due date calculated?
faq.a1: The estimated due date (EDD) is calculated using Naegele's rule: add 280 days (40 weeks) to the first day of the last menstrual period (LMP). This formula has been standard in obstetrics since the 19th century and assumes a regular 28-day cycle with ovulation on day 14. An alternative phrasing of the same rule: subtract 3 months from the LMP and add 7 days. For an LMP of January 10: subtract 3 months = October 10, add 7 days = October 17 (estimated due date). The 280-day count includes approximately 2 weeks before conception, which is why gestational age always runs 2 weeks ahead of fetal age.
faq.q2: How accurate is the due date?
faq.a2: Only approximately 4% of babies are born on their exact calculated due date. Normal full-term delivery spans weeks 37 to 42 of gestation — a 5-week biological window. Early first-trimester ultrasound (ideally between 8–12 weeks) is more accurate than LMP dating, with a margin of ±5–7 days versus ±14 days for LMP alone. When LMP-based dating and first-trimester ultrasound agree within 7 days, clinical practice uses the LMP date. When they differ by more than 7 days, most guidelines recommend accepting the ultrasound-derived EDD as more accurate, particularly for women with irregular cycles.
faq.q3: What are the three trimesters and what happens in each?
faq.a3: First Trimester (weeks 1–13): The embryonic period — all major organs form by week 10. The heart begins beating around week 6. The highest risk of miscarriage occurs in this period (approximately 10–15% of recognized pregnancies). First prenatal visit typically occurs at weeks 8–10. First-trimester screening (nuchal translucency ultrasound + blood tests for chromosomal conditions) is offered at weeks 11–13. Second Trimester (weeks 14–27): Generally the most comfortable period. Nausea often resolves. The fetus begins moving (felt by mother around weeks 18–22). The anatomy scan at week 20 checks fetal development in detail. Third Trimester (weeks 28–40+): Rapid fetal growth; lungs mature (by week 36). Weekly prenatal visits begin around week 36. Full-term threshold is 37 weeks; the due date is 40 weeks.
faq.a4: Gestational age ('weeks pregnant') is counted from the first day of the last menstrual period — not from conception. Since ovulation and conception typically occur around day 14 of the cycle, you are technically counted as 2 weeks pregnant at the moment of conception. A positive pregnancy test (usually detectable 4–5 weeks from LMP, about 2 weeks after conception) typically means you are already 4–5 gestational weeks pregnant. This counterintuitive counting system is universal in obstetrics because the LMP date is reliably known, while the exact conception date usually is not.
faq.q5: What if I don't know my last menstrual period date?
faq.a5: If you are unsure of your LMP, several alternatives exist. First, try to recall your approximate last period — even knowing the week is sufficient for an initial estimate within ±7 days. Second, use a conception date if you know it (select Conception Date mode). Third, use a known due date from a prior ultrasound or prenatal visit (select Known Due Date mode). Fourth, your healthcare provider can perform a first-trimester ultrasound to date the pregnancy directly from fetal crown-rump length — this is the most accurate method when LMP is unknown and is standard practice in the first prenatal visit when the date is uncertain.
faq.q6: What is the difference between this pregnancy calculator and the due date calculator?
faq.a6: Both calculators use Naegele's rule and produce the same core due date, gestational age, and trimester results. The due date calculator also supports IVF-specific transfer date methods (day-3 and day-5 embryo transfer) which are more accurate for IVF pregnancies. This pregnancy calculator supports a 'Known Due Date' input mode — useful when you already have an EDD from an ultrasound and want to work backwards to find the LMP or track your milestones from a confirmed date. Use whichever calculator's input method matches the date information you have.
faq.q7: When should I have my first prenatal appointment?
faq.a7: The first prenatal visit is ideally scheduled at 6–8 weeks gestation (about 2–4 weeks after a missed period, or 4–6 weeks after conception). At this visit, your provider confirms the pregnancy, establishes the EDD (via LMP and/or early ultrasound), screens for risk factors, discusses prenatal vitamins (folic acid is critical in the first 12 weeks for neural tube development), and sets the schedule for all subsequent screenings. Waiting until 12 weeks risks missing the optimal window for first-trimester chromosomal screening (nuchal translucency, done at 11–13+6 weeks) and delays establishing accurate dating.
faq.q8: What is a normal amount of weight to gain during pregnancy?
faq.a8: The American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) recommends gestational weight gain based on pre-pregnancy BMI. For normal BMI (18.5–24.9): 11.5–16 kg (25–35 lb) total. For overweight BMI (25–29.9): 7–11.5 kg (15–25 lb). For obese BMI (≥30): 5–9 kg (11–20 lb). For underweight BMI (<18.5): 12.5–18 kg (28–40 lb). Weight gain should be gradual — approximately 0.5–2 kg in the first trimester, then 0.3–0.5 kg per week in the second and third trimesters. Excessive gestational weight gain is associated with higher rates of cesarean delivery, gestational diabetes, and postpartum weight retention.

content.whatIs:
The pregnancy calculator estimates your due date, current gestational age in weeks and days, trimester, days remaining until birth, and a complete milestone timeline — from conception through key prenatal screenings to the expected delivery date. It supports three input methods: last menstrual period (LMP, the standard clinical approach), known conception date, or a confirmed due date from a prior ultrasound or provider visit.

Pregnancy is measured in gestational weeks counted from the first day of the last menstrual period, not from conception. This means that by the time a positive pregnancy test appears (typically 4–5 weeks from LMP), you are already about a month into your gestational count. The 40-week pregnancy framework based on Naegele's rule — the formula used by this calculator — has been the clinical standard since the early 1800s, and while ultrasound can refine the dating, the 40-week LMP framework remains the primary reference for scheduling all prenatal care.

Knowing your gestational age at any given moment is practically important: it tells you which trimester you are in, which prenatal screenings are due or upcoming, what fetal development milestones to expect, and how many days remain until your estimated delivery. This calculator provides all of these from a single date input, allowing you to quickly orient yourself in the pregnancy timeline at any stage.
content.formula:
From Last Menstrual Period (LMP):
  Due Date = LMP + 280 days
  Conception Date = LMP + 14 days

From Conception Date:
  Due Date = Conception + 266 days
  LMP equivalent = Conception − 14 days

From Known Due Date:
  LMP equivalent = Due Date − 280 days
  Conception Date = Due Date − 266 days

Gestational Age:
  Days pregnant = Today − LMP equivalent
  Weeks = Days ÷ 7 (rounded down)
  Remaining days = Days mod 7

Trimester Boundaries:
  First:  LMP to LMP+90 days (weeks 1–12+6)
  Second: LMP+91 to LMP+189 days (weeks 13–26+6)
  Third:  LMP+190 days onwards (week 27+)

Days remaining = Due Date − Today
content.example:
Example 1 — LMP input:
LMP: February 1, 2025
Due Date: February 1 + 280 days = November 8, 2025
Conception estimate: February 15, 2025
At week 12 (April 26, 2025): End of first trimester
At week 20 (June 21, 2025): Anatomy scan due
At week 28 (August 16, 2025): Third trimester begins
At week 37 (October 18, 2025): Full-term threshold

Example 2 — Known due date input:
Due Date provided: October 15, 2025
LMP equivalent: October 15 − 280 = January 8, 2025
Conception estimate: January 22, 2025
Current gestational age (if today = July 18, 2025):
  Days from LMP = 191 → 27 weeks 2 days → Third Trimester
  Days remaining: October 15 − July 18 = 89 days
content.howItWorks_1: Select your input method. 'Last Menstrual Period' is the most common — use the first day of your last period (day 1 of flow, not spotting). 'Conception Date' is useful if you know precisely when you conceived (e.g., from ovulation tracking or IUI). 'Known Due Date' works backwards from a confirmed EDD, useful when your provider established the due date via ultrasound.
content.howItWorks_2: Enter the date using the date picker or by typing in the date field. All calculations are relative to today's date, so the gestational age and days remaining update based on when you use the calculator.
content.howItWorks_3: Click Calculate to see your full pregnancy summary: Due Date, Weeks Pregnant (and days), Days Pregnant total, Days Until Due Date (or days overdue if past EDD), Trimester, estimated Conception Date, LMP date, and the milestone timeline. Use the milestone list to track upcoming screenings, developmental events, and trimester transitions.
content.whyItMatters:
Knowing your gestational age accurately is the foundation of all prenatal care scheduling. Every screening test in pregnancy has an optimal gestational window — outside that window, the test either cannot be performed or loses its clinical utility. The nuchal translucency scan for chromosomal abnormality detection must be performed between 11 weeks 0 days and 13 weeks 6 days — a 21-day window. The anatomy scan for fetal structural assessment is most informative at 18–22 weeks. Group B Streptococcus (GBS) testing is performed at 36–37 weeks to guide antibiotic use during labor. Missing these windows means waiting until the next pregnancy or relying on less accurate alternatives.

Beyond screening schedules, gestational age determines clinical thresholds for intervention. Fetal viability — the point at which a baby can survive outside the womb with medical support — is generally accepted at 24 weeks, with survival rates improving significantly by 28 weeks and approaching full-term rates by 34 weeks. Medical decisions about preterm labor management, steroid administration for lung maturation, and delivery timing all hinge on precise gestational age. The difference between 23 weeks 6 days and 24 weeks 0 days can determine the clinical approach to premature labor in a hospital setting.

For expectant parents, tracking pregnancy milestones provides the context to understand what is happening with fetal development at each stage. At 8 weeks, the fetus has all major organs beginning to form. At 20 weeks, the baby can hear sounds and begins REM sleep cycles. At 28 weeks, the eyes open. At 36 weeks, the lungs are mature enough for survival without intensive support in most cases. This developmental timeline — visible in the milestone output of this calculator — transforms abstract gestational age numbers into meaningful developmental context.
content.limitations:
This calculator applies Naegele's rule, which assumes a standard 28-day cycle with ovulation on day 14. Women with longer cycles (e.g., 35–40 days) ovulate later, meaning conception occurs later and the true due date is later than Naegele's rule would calculate. Women with shorter cycles (e.g., 21–24 days) ovulate earlier. The error from assuming a standard cycle can be 1–2 weeks. For women with known irregular cycles, first-trimester ultrasound dating is significantly more accurate than LMP-based calculation.

The milestone timeline uses typical gestational age windows for standard prenatal screening and developmental events. Individual pregnancies vary, and your healthcare provider may schedule or recommend different screening timelines based on your specific health history, risk factors, geographic location, and healthcare system. Some screenings (like cell-free DNA testing for chromosomal conditions) may be offered earlier or later than the standard windows shown. Always coordinate prenatal screening schedules with your OB/GYN or midwife.

This calculator does not assess pregnancy health, risk factors, or complications. It provides gestational dating information only. Any concerns about symptoms, fetal movement, bleeding, or pain during pregnancy should be addressed with a healthcare provider immediately — not estimated from a calculator. The calculator is a planning and information tool, not a medical assessment.
content.tips_1: Record the first day of your last period as soon as you suspect you might be pregnant, or ideally as part of regular cycle tracking. This date is the single most important piece of information for establishing your EDD and gestational age. If you are actively trying to conceive, a period-tracking app (Clue, Flo, Natural Cycles) automatically records this date and eliminates the memory requirement.
content.tips_2: Schedule your first prenatal appointment before 10 weeks gestation. Early care establishes accurate dating, begins important supplementation (folic acid 400–800 mcg/day reduces neural tube defect risk by 50–70%), screens for conditions that affect prenatal care (blood type, immune status, diabetes risk), and ensures you are in the system before time-sensitive first-trimester screening windows open at week 11.
content.tips_3: Use the milestone timeline as a conversation guide with your provider — not as a strict schedule. The listed weeks for anatomy scans, GBS testing, and prenatal visits are standard starting points. Your provider may adjust timing based on findings at each visit. Asking 'what should I expect in the next 4 weeks' at each appointment is more useful than comparing your experience to a generic milestone list.
content.tips_4: Plan for the full ±2 week window around your due date rather than preparing for a fixed date. Pack your hospital bag by week 36–37 (full-term threshold), complete parental leave paperwork by week 35, and arrange childcare or pet care coverage to begin at any time from week 38 onward. Flexibility planning reduces stress significantly — parents who have a fixed mental due date often experience unnecessary anxiety when babies arrive earlier or later than that exact day.
